title: MentalMARBERT: Domain-Adaptive Pre-training and Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Arabic Mental Health Disorders Detection
summary: Detecting mental health disorders from Arabic social media text remains challenging due to dialectal variation, informal language, limited high-quality annotated resources, and severe class imbalance. While English mental health natural language processing (NLP) has progressed substantially, Arabic multi-class disorder classification remains insufficiently studied. This study proposes a two-phase framework for Arabic mental health text classification. In phase 1, three Arabic pre-trained language models, AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, and MARBERT, undergo Domain-Adaptive and Task-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT and TAPT) using a large-scale corpus of unlabeled Arabic mental health tweets. The adapted models are evaluated under a unified protocol to identify the most effective backbone model. In phase 2, the selected model is assessed across four configurations combining single-stage and hierarchical two-stage classification architectures with full fine-tuning and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). To support this study, we constructed a novel annotated Arabic mental health dataset comprising 50,670 tweets across six categories, with strong inter annotator agreement (Krippendorff's Alpha = 0.733, average pairwise agreement = 0.797). Experimental results show that the domain-adapted MARBERT (MentalMARBERT) achieves statistically significant improvements over baseline models in both accuracy and macro-F1. The hierarchical two-stage architecture combined with full fine-tuning achieves the best overall performance, reaching a macro-F1 of 0.861 and an accuracy of 0.877. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of domain-specific adaptive pretraining and hierarchical classification for Arabic mental health disorder detection.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a two-phase framework for detecting multiple mental health disorders in Arabic Twitter data using adapted pre-trained language models and hierarchical classification, achieving strong performance on a newly created large annotated dataset.
AI.motivation: Arabic mental health disorder detection from social media is difficult due to dialect diversity, informal language, lack of annotated data, and class imbalance. Existing NLP work focuses mostly on English and often on binary classification, leaving a gap in multi-class Arabic mental health detection. The authors aim to address this gap and improve performance by tailoring modern Arabic PLMs to the mental health domain and by designing effective classification architectures.
AI.method: Phase 1: The authors take three Arabic PLMs (AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, MARBERT) and further pretrain them with Domain-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT) and Task-Adaptive Pretraining (TAPT) on a large corpus of unlabeled Arabic mental health tweets. They then compare the adapted models under a unified evaluation protocol to select the best backbone. Phase 2: Using the best model (MARBERT adapted to the mental health domain, named MentalMARBERT), they test four setups combining: (a) single-stage vs hierarchical two-stage classification architectures; and (b) full fine-tuning vs parameter-efficient Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). They also build a new labeled dataset of 50,670 Arabic tweets annotated into six mental health-related categories with high inter-annotator agreement.
AI.result: Domain-adapted MARBERT (MentalMARBERT) outperforms other baselines (including other adapted PLMs) with statistically significant gains in both accuracy and macro-F1. Among the architectural and training configurations, the hierarchical two-stage classifier with full fine-tuning gives the best overall performance, achieving macro-F1 of 0.861 and accuracy of 0.877 on the six-way mental health classification task.
AI.conclusion: Adapting Arabic PLMs to the mental health domain via DAPT and TAPT substantially improves multi-class disorder classification on Arabic tweets, especially when combined with a hierarchical two-stage architecture and full fine-tuning. The newly introduced large annotated dataset and the MentalMARBERT model establish strong baselines and demonstrate that domain-specific pretraining and hierarchical modeling are effective strategies for Arabic mental health disorder detection from social media.

title: AfriSUD: A Dependency Treebank Collection for Evaluating Models on African Languages
summary: Despite their linguistic diversity and global significance, African languages remain underrepresented in research and resources to support NLP. We aim to bridge this gap by introducing AfriSUD, the first large-scale collection of syntactically annotated treebanks for nine diverse African languages spanning major language families and regions across Sub-Saharan Africa. Using the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) framework, our community-led effort provides high-quality, native-speaker verified data that capture typological key features such as agglutination and tone. We evaluate a range of models on AfriSUD for part-of-speech tagging and dependency parsing including non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders, and LLMs. Our results reveal a significant syntax gap, where models still show clear limitations across the nine languages, suggesting that existing architectures may not fully capture the structural diversity of African-language syntax.

AI.tldr: AfriSUD introduces the first large-scale syntactically annotated treebanks for nine African languages, evaluates multiple POS-tagging and dependency-parsing models, and shows a persistent syntax gap for these languages.
AI.motivation: African languages are linguistically diverse and globally important yet severely underrepresented in NLP research and resources. This lack of resources hinders the development of robust language technologies and limits inclusion for African-language speakers. The paper is motivated by the need to create high-quality, typologically aware datasets that support syntactic modeling for a wide range of African languages and to empirically assess how existing models handle their structural diversity.
AI.method: The authors build AfriSUD, a collection of syntactically annotated treebanks for nine African languages using the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) framework. The data is created via a community-led process with native-speaker verification, and the annotations are designed to capture key typological features like agglutination and tone. They then run systematic experiments on part-of-speech tagging and dependency parsing, comparing non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders, and large language models on the new treebanks.
AI.result: Across all nine African languages, the evaluated models underperform compared to expectations and to performance commonly seen on higher-resourced languages. The experiments reveal that both traditional models and modern multilingual transformers and LLMs struggle with POS tagging and dependency parsing, highlighting clear weaknesses in modeling the syntactic structures present in these languages.
AI.conclusion: The creation of AfriSUD fills a critical resource gap by providing the first broad-coverage, syntactically annotated treebanks for multiple African languages. The empirical results demonstrate a substantial syntax gap: current model architectures and training regimes do not adequately capture the structural diversity of African-language syntax. This underscores the need for new modeling approaches, better training data, and continued community-led efforts to support NLP for African languages.

title: "Did you lie?" Evaluating Lie Detectors across Model Scale and Belief-Verified Model Organisms
summary: Robust lie detectors for language models could enable powerful techniques for auditing, monitoring, and post-hoc investigation of model behaviour, but evaluating them requires testbeds where models verifiably believe the opposite of what they say. We show that existing trained model organisms often fail this requirement, leaving prior positive and negative detection results difficult to interpret. We address this with 13 reasoning model organisms whose hidden beliefs are verified in chain-of-thought and shown to generalise to held-out tasks, alongside Varied Deception, a prompted-lying testbed covering a broad range of lie-inducing motivations. On these testbeds we evaluate four detectors: a chain-of-thought judge, a logprob classifier, and two activation probes, including Did-You-Lie (DYL), a new method for training follow-up probes. On prompted lying, across 31 open-weight models spanning 2B to 1T parameters, all four detectors show positive scaling with model capability. However, every activation- and logprob-based detector drops sharply on our trained model organisms, with DYL retaining the most signal; only the chain-of-thought judge remains strong, with 0.82 balanced accuracy, partly as an artefact of our verification process favouring CoT-readable beliefs. Current lie detectors therefore cannot support high-confidence claims about model beliefs, and we suggest research directions that may address some of their current limitations. We release our datasets, model organisms, and trained detectors.

AI.tldr: Paper studies how to robustly detect when language models are lying, and finds that most current detectors are unreliable, especially on trained deceptive model organisms.
AI.motivation: Reliable lie detection for LMs would help auditing, safety, and post‑hoc analysis, but it is hard to evaluate detectors because we rarely have ground truth about what the model actually "believes" versus what it outputs. Existing benchmarks using trained deceptive models often don’t truly guarantee that internal beliefs contradict outputs, making previous results hard to interpret.
AI.method: 1) Build 13 "reasoning model organisms"—specialized models or setups—where internal chain‑of‑thought (CoT) makes their hidden beliefs observable and validated, and test that these beliefs generalize to new tasks. 2) Construct Varied Deception, a broad prompted‑lying benchmark with diverse motivations for lying. 3) Evaluate four lie detectors on both benchmarks: (a) a CoT‑based judge that inspects reasoning traces, (b) a log‑probability classifier that uses token likelihoods, and (c,d) two activation probes, one being a new method called Did‑You‑Lie (DYL) that trains follow‑up probes on model activations to detect deception. 4) Test these detectors across 31 open‑weight models from 2B to 1T parameters, measuring performance and scaling trends.
AI.result: On the Varied Deception prompted‑lying benchmark, all four detectors improve as base model size/capability increases. But on the more stringent reasoning model organisms, performance of activation‑ and logprob‑based detectors drops sharply, with DYL performing best among them but still degraded. Only the CoT‑judge remains strong, achieving around 0.82 balanced accuracy, though this is partly due to the benchmark design privileging CoT‑readable beliefs. This reveals a large gap between apparent success on looser benchmarks and genuine detection of mismatches between beliefs and outputs.
AI.conclusion: State‑of‑the‑art lie detectors for LMs are far from robust: they generalize poorly to settings where we can more confidently know the model’s internal beliefs, and their successes on standard prompted‑lying benchmarks can be misleading. Chain‑of‑thought‑based judges work relatively better but benefit from biases in how ground truth is defined. Thus, current methods cannot justify high‑confidence claims about model beliefs, and new research is needed—including better testbeds, more belief‑aligned supervision, and improved probing methods—to reliably detect deception in advanced language models.

title: LLMs Can Better Capture Human Judgments--With the Right Prompts
summary: Are large language models (LLMs) bad at capturing human judgment? Two commonly stated limitations are that LLMs fail to capture full distributions of responses, and that their judgments are unstable across wording variations. We demonstrate simple prompting strategies that mitigate these limitations. Across two datasets--a U.S.-representative set of 144 moral scenarios and 38 moral beliefs from the International Social Survey Programme's Family and Changing Gender Roles module covering 32 countries--we show how simple elicitation techniques help improve AI-human alignment. First, prompting models to report standard deviations and response proportions recovers the full range of human responses better than common strategies. Second, ensuring scenarios are clear to human participants--as reflected in human confusion ratings--boosts model alignment, and LLMs can track human confusion ratings. At the same time, we find that LLMs' estimates of their own error are poorly calibrated, though they can predict human variability relatively well. These results suggest that asking better questions to LLMs can yield better answers.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to better align large language model judgments with human moral judgments using simple prompting strategies and scenario clarifications.
AI.motivation: There is concern that large language models do not accurately reflect human judgments, especially in domains like moral decision-making. Two notable issues are that models often fail to reproduce the full distribution of human responses and that their outputs can be unstable when prompts are worded differently. Understanding and mitigating these gaps is important if LLM outputs are to be used as proxies for or complements to human judgment in social science, policy, or product settings.
AI.method: The authors evaluate prompting strategies on two datasets: (1) a U.S.-representative dataset of 144 moral scenarios, and (2) 38 moral belief items from the ISSP Family and Changing Gender Roles module across 32 countries. They test simple elicitation techniques where models are prompted to output distributions of responses (e.g., proportions, standard deviations) rather than single-point answers. They also analyze the effect of scenario clarity, using human confusion ratings as a measure, and examine whether LLMs can predict these confusion ratings. Finally, they compare LLMs’ self-estimated error and predicted human variability to actual human data to assess calibration and variability tracking.
AI.result: Prompting LLMs to output standard deviations and response proportions significantly improves their ability to match the full range and distribution of human responses compared to more common single-answer prompting. When scenarios are clearer to humans—indicated by lower confusion in human ratings—LLM judgments align more closely with human judgments, and LLMs are able to track human confusion levels themselves. However, LLMs’ own error estimates are poorly calibrated: their confidence or self-assessed error does not match how wrong they actually are, even though they can predict human variability around judgments relatively well.
AI.conclusion: Simple changes in how questions are asked of LLMs—such as eliciting distributions and accounting for scenario clarity—can substantially improve alignment between LLM and human moral judgments. While LLMs remain poorly calibrated about their own error, they are comparatively good at predicting variability in human responses. This suggests that thoughtful prompt design and attention to item clarity can make LLMs more reliable tools for approximating or studying human judgment, especially in moral and attitudinal domains.

title: Evoflux: Inference-Time Evolution of Executable Tool Workflows for Compact Agents
summary: Compact language models (LMs) reduce cost, latency, and deployment risk for tool agents. Yet MCP-style tool use requires more than isolated function calling: an agent must discover tools from live catalogs, satisfy schemas, preserve dependencies across intermediate outputs, and ground final responses in executed evidence. Small planners often generate plausible workflow graphs that fail under tool resolution, parameter validation, dependency tracking, or execution. We argue that this failure mode is poorly handled by small-corpus distillation. A few hundred teacher traces can teach workflow format, but rarely cover the recovery behavior needed to repair failed plans over changing tool catalogs. We introduce Evoflux, an inference-time evolutionary search method that treats compact tool use as the repair of executable tool workflows. It evolves typed workflow graphs through structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning. On held-out MCP-Bench tasks spanning live MCP servers and 250 tools, Evoflux raises execution feasibility from roughly 3% to 17-24% across small planners. In contrast, SFT and SFT+DPO on the same search-mined data match, underperform, or collapse below zero-shot performance; ReAct reaches higher peaks, but with higher variance and token cost. These results show that execution-grounded search is more reliable under scarce teacher-trace budgets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Evoflux, an inference-time evolutionary search method that makes compact language models more reliable tool agents by repairing and evolving executable tool workflows, significantly improving execution feasibility over prior training-based approaches under limited supervision.
AI.motivation: Compact language models are attractive for tool agents because they are cheaper, faster, and safer to deploy, but they struggle with complex, MCP-style tool use that requires discovering tools from live catalogs, satisfying schemas, tracking dependencies, and grounding responses in executed evidence. Small models often produce plausible-looking tool workflows that break during actual execution, and existing small-corpus distillation (a few hundred teacher traces) teaches them the basic format but not how to recover from or repair failed plans, especially as tool catalogs change. The authors aim to address this gap: how to make small planners robust to execution-time failures when supervised traces are scarce and tools are evolving.
AI.method: The authors introduce Evoflux, an inference-time evolutionary search algorithm that views tool use as a problem of repairing and improving executable tool workflows. Evoflux operates over typed workflow graphs and applies structured edits to them, guided by execution feedback (e.g., validation errors, tool failures), and adjusts search intensity adaptively based on progress. It also includes meta-guided redesign steps and diversity pruning to avoid converging on redundant or unproductive workflow variants. Rather than relying primarily on additional training data, Evoflux uses this evolutionary search at inference time to iteratively refine tool plans generated by compact LMs until they satisfy tool schemas, resolve dependencies, and execute successfully.
AI.result: On the MCP-Bench benchmark, which includes tasks over live MCP servers and roughly 250 tools, Evoflux substantially improves the execution feasibility of tool workflows generated by small planners: from about 3% baseline feasibility to approximately 17–24%, depending on the planner. In comparison, fine-tuning approaches such as standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and SFT combined with DPO, even when trained on data mined from the search process, either only match, underperform, or in some cases fall below the planners’ zero-shot performance. A ReAct-style approach can sometimes reach higher best-case performance but suffers from higher variance and greater token usage. Overall, Evoflux delivers more consistent gains with lower overhead for compact models.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that execution-grounded evolutionary search at inference time is an effective way to make compact language models more reliable tool agents under tight supervision budgets. Instead of relying on large sets of teacher traces, Evoflux leverages live execution feedback to iteratively repair and evolve workflow graphs, significantly increasing the rate of feasible tool executions across diverse, changing tool catalogs. The authors argue that such search-based, execution-aware methods are more robust and data-efficient than traditional finetuning approaches for enabling complex, MCP-style tool use in small models.

title: How Fine-Grained Should a RAG Benchmark Be? A Hierarchical Framework for Synthetic Question Generation
summary: Evaluating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems requires benchmarks that capture diverse question characteristics, yet practitioners lack empirical guidance on which dimensions to vary and at what granularity. We present HieraRAG, a hierarchical framework for studying granularity in RAG benchmark construction, defining optimal granularity as the level that maximizes discriminative power (the standard deviation of generation quality across categories) within a given RAG configuration. As a case study, we generate 5,872 synthetic question-answer (QA) pairs from FineWeb-10BT across 3 dimensions (Question Complexity, Answer Type, Linguistic Variation) at 3 granularity levels (2, 4, and 8 categories). With a BM25+Falcon-3-10B pipeline, optimal granularity varies by dimension: complexity benefits from fine-grained distinctions (discriminative power: 0.053) while answer type and linguistic variation peak at medium granularity. We introduce a Coherence Ratio metric to quantify whether fine-grained splits cleanly subdivide parent categories, revealing structural differences across dimensions (Question Complexity: 0.40 vs. Answer Type: 1.44). Human evaluation of 110 stratified QA pairs confirms synthetic quality. While these specific findings reflect a single configuration, HieraRAG provides a portable procedure and validation metric for practitioners to determine evaluation granularity within their own RAG settings.

AI.tldr: HieraRAG is a hierarchical framework to decide how fine-grained RAG evaluation benchmarks should be, by picking the granularity that best separates performance across question categories.
AI.motivation: RAG systems need good benchmarks that reflect diverse question characteristics, but practitioners lack empirical guidance on which dimensions (e.g., question complexity, answer type) to vary and how finely to split them into categories. Without this, benchmarks may be either too coarse to distinguish systems or too fine to be meaningful or coherent.
AI.method: They define a notion of optimal granularity as the category resolution that maximizes discriminative power (measured as standard deviation of generation quality across categories) for a fixed RAG setup. They build a hierarchical framework, HieraRAG, and as a case study generate 5,872 synthetic QA pairs from FineWeb-10BT along three dimensions—Question Complexity, Answer Type, and Linguistic Variation—each at three granularity levels of 2, 4, and 8 categories. Using a specific BM25+Falcon-3-10B pipeline, they measure generation quality and compute discriminative power per granularity and dimension. They also introduce a Coherence Ratio metric to test whether finer-grained splits meaningfully and cleanly subdivide their parent categories. Human evaluation on a stratified subset validates the synthetic QA quality.
AI.result: For the studied BM25+Falcon-3-10B RAG configuration, the optimal granularity differs per dimension: question complexity benefits from the finest 8-category split (with a reported discriminative power of 0.053), whereas answer type and linguistic variation achieve maximum discriminative power at an intermediate 4-category level. The Coherence Ratio further shows that the hierarchical splits behave differently across dimensions; complexity has lower coherence (0.40), suggesting noisier or less clean subcategories, while answer type has higher coherence (1.44), indicating more structurally consistent splits. Human evaluation of 110 QA pairs confirms that the synthetic benchmark data is of acceptable quality.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that evaluation granularity in RAG benchmarks is dimension-dependent rather than one-size-fits-all: different aspects like complexity, answer type, and linguistic variation each have their own optimal level of category refinement. HieraRAG offers a general, portable procedure plus a validation metric (Coherence Ratio) that practitioners can apply within their own RAG setups to empirically choose granularity, rather than relying on ad hoc or fixed category designs.

title: Small LLMs for Biomedical Claim Verification: Cost-Effective Fine-Tuning, Structural Dataset Shortcuts, and Cross-Domain Generalization
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 12 tables. To appear at BioNLP Workshop, ACL 2026
summary: Large Language Models such as GPT-4o and GPT-5 achieve strong zero-shot performance on biomedical claim verification, but cost and opacity limit scalable use. We fine-tune three small LLMs: Phi-3-mini (3.8B), Qwen2.5-3B, and Mistral-7B, via QLoRA on SciFact and HealthVer, providing the first study of QLoRA models against GPT-4o and fine-tuned BioLinkBERT encoders. Mistral-7B QLoRA surpasses both GPT-4o and GPT-5 (up to 12% F1 gain) at a fractional cost using just 1,008 training examples. We conduct extensive in-domain and cross-domain evaluation: models trained on SciFact tested on HealthVer and vice versa, at matched sizes to isolate dataset structure from data quantity. We identify a previously unreported structural artifact in SciFact that inflates in-domain scores, and show through bidirectional out-of-domain evaluation that training on structurally sound data enables robust cross-domain transfer. We plan to release all code and adapter checkpoints.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that small LLMs fine-tuned with QLoRA can outperform larger closed models like GPT-4o and GPT-5 for biomedical claim verification, at much lower cost, and reveals a structural artifact in the SciFact dataset that inflates in-domain results.
AI.motivation: Large closed LLMs perform well on biomedical claim verification but are expensive, opaque, and hard to deploy at scale. There is a need to know whether small, open, fine-tuned models can match or exceed their performance, and to understand how dataset structure affects evaluation, especially across domains.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune three small open LLMs (Phi-3-mini 3.8B, Qwen2.5-3B, Mistral-7B) using QLoRA on two biomedical fact-checking datasets (SciFact and HealthVer). They then conduct systematic in-domain and cross-domain evaluations, including training on one dataset and testing on the other at matched data sizes, and compare against GPT-4o, GPT-5, and a fine-tuned BioLinkBERT encoder, to disentangle dataset structure effects from data quantity.
AI.result: Mistral-7B fine-tuned with QLoRA outperforms GPT-4o and GPT-5 on biomedical claim verification by up to 12 F1 points using only 1,008 training examples and at a small fraction of the inference and training cost. The comparative experiments reveal that SciFact contains a structural artifact that boosts in-domain scores, and that models trained on structurally cleaner data yield better cross-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: Small, open LLMs fine-tuned with parameter-efficient methods like QLoRA can be both more accurate and vastly cheaper than large proprietary LLMs for specialized tasks such as biomedical claim verification. Reliable cross-domain performance depends critically on the structural quality of training data: artifacts in widely used benchmarks like SciFact can mislead evaluations, whereas training on structurally sound datasets supports robust transfer. The authors intend to release their code and adapter checkpoints to facilitate reproduction and further research.

title: Prefill Awareness in Large Language Models
summary: Safety-relevant studies of language models, including alignment and jailbreaking evaluations and AI control protocols, often rely on prefilling model outputs. If AI models can recognize and act on the fact their prior assistant messages have been inserted or edited, the effectiveness and validity of these methods could be compromised. We investigate whether frontier language models can distinguish between tampered and untampered assistant-side context, a capability we call prefill awareness. To do so, we construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms, filtering for cases where models show consistent stances. We find that frontier models show substantial prefill awareness: Claude Opus 4.5 detects prefills opposing its preferences in 9-35% of cases with a 0% false positive rate when prompted; additionally, models often revert towards baseline behavior without explicitly reporting that the prefill was foreign. Controlled ablations later also show that detection and resistance rely on different cues, where stylistic mismatch mainly affects whether models flag a prefill as foreign, while preference mismatch mainly affects whether they revert toward their baseline answer. We also examine more realistic agentic settings such as misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories, where frontier models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns in ways that depend strongly on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts. Our results indicate that prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based methods. We recommend that model developers track this capability in frontier systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether advanced language models can detect when their previous assistant messages have been inserted or modified ("prefill awareness"), showing that current frontier models often can, which undermines some common safety evaluation methods.
AI.motivation: Many safety and alignment evaluations, as well as AI control protocols, assume that language models passively accept prefilled assistant-side context (e.g., prewritten answers or partial trajectories). If models can recognize and react to such tampering, these prefill-based methods may yield misleading results about model alignment, robustness, and controllability. The authors are motivated to test whether frontier models have this prefill awareness capability and how it affects evaluations.
AI.method: The authors define "prefill awareness" as the ability of a model to distinguish tampered from untampered assistant-side context and respond differently. They construct a binary preference benchmark using three different prefill mechanisms and filter for examples where models have consistent baseline stances. They then measure detection rates and behavior reversion rates for various frontier models (e.g., Claude Opus 4.5). They also run controlled ablations to separate the effects of stylistic mismatch versus preference mismatch on detection and resistance. Finally, they probe more realistic agentic scenarios (e.g., misalignment-continuation setups and SWE-bench code trajectories) to observe how often and under what conditions models disavow or override prefilled content.
AI.result: Frontier language models show notable prefill awareness. Claude Opus 4.5, for instance, detects prefills that contradict its preferences in 9–35% of evaluated cases with a 0% false positive rate when explicitly prompted to check. Even when models do not explicitly report the prefill as foreign, they frequently revert toward their baseline answers. Ablation experiments indicate that stylistic mismatch mainly drives whether the model flags a prefill as foreign, while preference mismatch mainly drives whether the model overrides it and returns to its baseline behavior. In more realistic agentic settings such as misalignment-continuation tasks and SWE-bench trajectories, models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns, with the frequency and nature of this behavior depending heavily on the specific dataset, task performance, and even subtle formatting artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that prefill awareness already exists to a meaningful degree in current frontier language models and constitutes a significant confound for methods that rely on uncritical acceptance of prefilled content, such as many alignment and jailbreaking evaluations or AI control protocols. Because models can detect and sometimes resist or disavow prefills, these evaluations may not reliably measure underlying preferences or controllability. The authors recommend that model developers explicitly monitor and track prefill awareness as a capability in frontier systems and account for its impact when designing and interpreting prefill-based safety and alignment methodologies.

title: LAUKIN: A Multi-jurisdictional Common Law Contract Dataset
summary: Multinational companies increasingly require cross-jurisdictional contract review, yet existing legal NLP datasets are largely restricted to a single jurisdiction. We introduce LAUKIN (Legal equivalence dataset of Australia, UK, and INdia), a dataset of clause pairs (AU-UK, UK-IN, IN-AU) labelled for boolean legal equivalence. We develop a novel multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline to construct the initial clause pair mapping, with a subset of clause pairs subsequently annotated by legal experts as Equivalent or Not Equivalent. The dataset comprises 14,727 clause pairs from 204 contracts across 8 agreement types, of which 3,000 are manually labelled: 900 train, 600 dev, and 1,500 test. We evaluate 12 models across 4 techniques, achieving a best macro-F1 of 65.11%, establishing LAUKIN as a challenging benchmark. Results reveal that, despite shared legal heritage, drafting conventions diverge significantly across jurisdictions, making cross-jurisdictional equivalence classification non-trivial. LAUKIN also includes 11,727 unlabelled training pairs to support future semi-supervised learning research in legal NLP.

AI.tldr: Introduces LAUKIN, a new cross-jurisdictional legal clause equivalence dataset (AU, UK, IN) and shows it is a challenging benchmark for legal NLP models.
AI.motivation: Multinational companies need contract review across multiple legal jurisdictions, but existing legal NLP datasets are usually confined to a single jurisdiction, limiting the development and evaluation of models that must understand legal equivalence across different countries’ laws and drafting styles.
AI.method: The authors construct LAUKIN, a dataset of clause pairs between Australia, the UK, and India, using a multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline to identify candidate equivalent clauses across contracts and jurisdictions. A subset of these candidate pairs is then manually annotated by legal experts as legally Equivalent or Not Equivalent. The final dataset contains 14,727 clause pairs from 204 contracts and 8 agreement types, with 3,000 pairs manually labelled and split into train/dev/test, plus 11,727 additional unlabelled pairs. They evaluate 12 models using four different technical approaches on the binary equivalence classification task.
AI.result: The best-performing approach achieves a macro-F1 score of 65.11% on the legal equivalence classification task, indicating that current models find the dataset challenging. Analysis shows that even among jurisdictions with a shared legal heritage (Australia, UK, India), drafting conventions differ enough to make cross-jurisdictional clause equivalence a non-trivial problem.
AI.conclusion: LAUKIN is established as a challenging and realistic benchmark for cross-jurisdictional legal equivalence classification. The dataset exposes the limitations of current models in handling variation in legal drafting across related jurisdictions, and the inclusion of a large pool of unlabelled pairs offers opportunities for future semi-supervised and other advanced learning approaches in legal NLP.

title: Multi-Modal Agents for Power Distribution Defect Detection: An Evaluation of Foundation Models
summary: The power distribution network is critical to reliable electricity delivery, yet traditional inspection methods face limitations in semantic understanding, generalization, and closed-loop automation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Multi-Modal Agent framework specifically for power distribution defect detection. Central to this study is the systematic evaluation of multimodal foundation models as unified cognitive engines. We rigorously assess their integrated performance across three critical capabilities: (1) Perception, where the model must accurately identify equipment and generate expert-level descriptions of defects; (2) Reasoning, where the model interprets visual findings to diagnose causes, assess severity, and plan maintenance strategies based on domain knowledge; and (3) Tool Usage, where the model acts as an autonomous operator to execute actions -- such as querying knowledge bases or generating work orders -- to achieve closed-loop maintenance. To support this evaluation, a domain-specific evaluation dataset and a comprehensive benchmark are developed. Experimental results demonstrate the strengths and limitations of current foundation models in these three dimensions, providing empirical evidence for deploying autonomous agents in high-stakes industrial environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and evaluates a multi-modal agent framework, powered by multimodal foundation models, for automated defect detection and maintenance in power distribution networks.
AI.motivation: Power distribution networks require reliable inspection, but existing methods struggle with deep semantic understanding of defects, generalization to diverse scenarios, and enabling closed-loop automation from detection to maintenance. With the emergence of multimodal foundation models, there is a need to explore whether they can serve as unified cognitive engines that perceive, reason, and act autonomously in high-stakes industrial environments.
AI.method: The authors design a Multi-Modal Agent framework tailored to power distribution defect detection. They treat multimodal foundation models as the core cognitive module and systematically evaluate them along three key capabilities: (1) Perception: identifying equipment and describing defects at an expert level from visual inputs; (2) Reasoning: diagnosing causes, assessing severity, and planning maintenance using domain knowledge; (3) Tool Usage: autonomously invoking external tools such as knowledge bases and work-order systems to close the loop from detection to maintenance. To enable rigorous evaluation, they build a domain-specific dataset and a comprehensive benchmark targeting these three dimensions.
AI.result: Through experiments on the constructed benchmark, the paper reveals both the strengths and weaknesses of existing multimodal foundation models in perception, reasoning, and tool usage for power distribution scenarios. The models can handle many perception tasks and some reasoning tasks but show limitations in complex diagnosis, nuanced severity assessment, and robust autonomous tool invocation. Performance metrics across the three dimensions provide a detailed picture of current capabilities and gaps.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that multimodal foundation models show promising potential as unified cognitive engines for power distribution defect detection and maintenance, but they are not yet fully reliable for completely autonomous operation in high-stakes settings. The proposed Multi-Modal Agent framework, dataset, and benchmark offer a foundation for future research and practical deployment, guiding improvements in perception, reasoning, and tool-use capabilities needed for safe and effective industrial agents.
